prs.title: feat: Add graph_ir column + registry-driven code generator (CP1 gate)

labels.name: needs-revision
labels.description: Reviewed; changes requested before it can land

prs.body:
This PR delivers the Week 3 **CP1 Gate** milestone, implementing a complete database migration and code generator refactor that enables all 15 layer types to work end-to-end from canvas to code generation.

### Key Changes

1. **Database Migration**
   - Add `graph_ir` JSON column to `model_basic` table
   - Implement dual-write strategy (writes to both `graph_ir` and legacy `model_configs`)
   - Dual-read fallback (reads from `graph_ir` → `graph_json` → `model_configs`)
   - Backfill script to populate `graph_ir` for existing models

2. **Code Generator Refactor**
   - New `TensorFlowGenerator` class with registry-driven layer construction
   - Replaces hardcoded 60+ line elif chain with clean registry lookups
   - Lazy TensorFlow imports (no import-time overhead)
   - BFS traversal for topological ordering
   - Merge layer support (Concatenate)

3. **Layer Registry Enhancements**
   - All 15 layer types fully registered with metadata
   - Legacy format support (automatic conversion of old ReactFlow formats)
   - Parameter validation and type safety

### Test Coverage

```
tests/test_migration.py ......... 8 tests ✓
tests/test_cp1.py ............... 38 tests ✓
tests/test_registry.py .......... 25 tests ✓
─────────────────────────────────────────
TOTAL: 71 tests passing
```

### Architecture

**Before (Week 2):**
```
ReactFlow JSON → model_configs (KV table, 50+ rows) → hardcoded elif chain
```

**After (Week 3):**
```
ReactFlow JSON → IRGraph → graph_ir (JSON column) → TensorFlowGenerator (registry-driven)
                 ↓
           model_configs (backward compat, dual-write)
```


### Highlights

- **15 layer types**: Input, Dense, Flatten, Conv2D, MaxPool2D, AvgPool2D, GlobalAvgPool2D, LSTM, GRU, SimpleRNN, Embedding, Dropout, BatchNormalization, Reshape, Concatenate
- **Zero downtime**: Dual-write ensures no data loss
- **Fast tests**: Lazy TF import = sub-second test suite
- **Clean architecture**: Registry pattern replaces elif chains


**CP1 Gate Achieved!** All 15 layer types work end-to-end.

labels.name: needs-revision
labels.description: Reviewed; changes requested before it can land

prs.body: ## Summary Implements a registry-driven frontend that dynamically renders all 15 neural network layer types from the backend `/api/layers/` endpoint. Replaces hardcoded layer components with a universal `GenericLayerNode` and adds a categorized, searchable sidebar. ## Changes ### Core Components #### 1. **GenericLayerNode** (`src/components/nodes/GenericLayerNode.tsx`) - Universal node component rendering all 15 layer types from registry - Category-based color coding (7 categories: core, convolutional, recurrent, regularization, pooling, encoding, utility) - Two-letter abbreviation badges for quick identification - Dynamic parameter summary (shows top 2 params) - Support for merge layers (Concatenate) with multiple input handles - Skeleton loading state and error handling #### 2. **Categorized Sidebar** (`src/components/DragAndDropCanvas/Sidebar.tsx`) - Displays all 15 layers organized by 7 categories - Real-time search across all categories (case-insensitive) - Collapsible category sections with layer count badges - Draggable layer items with registry-driven tooltips - Auto-expands all categories during search #### 3. **Dynamic NodePropertiesPanel** (`src/components/DragAndDropCanvas/NodePropertiesPanel.tsx`) - Auto-generates form fields from ParamSpec - Supports 4 parameter types: `int`, `float`, `bool`, `enum` - Real-time validation with error messages - Required field indicators and description tooltips - "Reset to Defaults" button #### 4. **Registry-Driven Validation** (`src/components/DragAndDropCanvas/Helpers.ts`) - Removed all hardcoded switch statements - Validates all 15 layer types using registry specs - Checks required parameters, min/max constraints - Validates merge layer connections (Concatenate needs ≥2 inputs) - Returns detailed error messages ### Infrastructure #### 5. **useLayerRegistry Hook** (`src/hooks/useLayerRegistry.ts`) - Fetches registry from `/api/v1/layers/` endpoint - Module-level caching (single API call per session) - Helper functions: `getLayerSpec()`, `getAllLayerSpecs()` - Handles loading and error states #### 6. **Type Definitions** (`src/types/registry.ts`) - TypeScript interfaces matching backend schema - `ParamSpec`, `LayerSpec`, `LayerRegistryResponse` - Category color mappings and layer abbreviations #### 7. **Backward Compatibility** (`src/components/nodes/LegacyNodeWrapper.tsx`) - Wrapper factory for existing node types - Maps old custom types to new registry types - Preserves existing saved models ### Canvas Integration #### 8. **Updated Canvas** (`src/components/DragAndDropCanvas/Canvas.jsx`) - Added `genericlayer` to nodeTypes map - Registry-driven `onDrop` handler with default params - Maintains backward compatibility with existing custom nodes ### Code Quality - **TypeScript Migration**: Converted 3 components (Sidebar, NodePropertiesPanel, Helpers) from JSX to TSX - **Test Coverage**: Added 25 comprehensive tests across 4 test files - **ESLint & Prettier**: Zero errors, all files formatted ## Screenshot ### Before (Hardcoded) - 7 hardcoded layer types in sidebar - Individual node components (InputNode, DenseNode, etc.) - Hardcoded validation switch statements - No search functionality ### After (Registry-Driven) - 15 layer types organized in 7 categories - Single GenericLayerNode for all types - Searchable, collapsible sidebar - Dynamic property editor - Registry-based validation - Category color coding

prs.title: feat(backend): Week 1 Registry Schema and Layers API

labels.name: needs-revision
labels.description: Reviewed; changes requested before it can land

prs.body:
This PR implements the foundational backend infrastructure for TensorMap's neural network builder, establishing a centralized layer registry system and intermediate representation (IR) schema for graph-based model construction.

## Objectives

- **Registry-Driven Architecture**: Eliminate hardcoded if/elif chains by centralizing layer definitions in a maintainable registry
- **Type-Safe IR Schema**: Define Pydantic models for representing neural networks as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs)
- **REST API Endpoints**: Expose layer registry and graph validation through FastAPI endpoints
- **Graph Validation**: Implement topology validation with cycle detection and constraint checking

## What's New

### 1. Layer Registry System (`app/layers/registry.py`)

A centralized registry defining 15 neural network layer types across 7 categories:

**Categories:**
- **Core**: Input, Dense, Flatten
- **Convolutional**: Conv2D
- **Pooling**: MaxPooling2D, AveragePooling2D, GlobalAveragePooling2D
- **Recurrent**: LSTM, GRU, SimpleRNN
- **Regularization**: Dropout, BatchNormalization
- **Encoding**: Embedding
- **Utility**: Reshape, Concatenate

**Key Components:**
- `ActivationType`: Enum of 8 activation functions (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh, etc.)
- `ParamType`: Parameter type system (int, float, bool, enum)
- `ParamSpec`: Parameter specification with validation constraints (min, max, values)
- `LayerSpec`: Complete layer specification with Keras class mapping
- `LAYER_REGISTRY`: Central dictionary mapping layer type keys to specifications

**Helper Functions:**
- `get_layer_spec(type_key)`: Retrieve layer specification by type
- `serialize_registry()`: Convert registry to JSON-serializable format
- `get_layers_by_category()`: Group layers by category for UI organization

### 2. Intermediate Representation (IR) Schema (`app/ir/schema.py`)

Pydantic models for representing neural network graphs:

**Schema Structure:**
```
IRGraph
├── version: str
├── nodes: list[IRNode]
└── edges: list[IREdge]
```

**Node Parameters (Discriminated Union):**
- 15 layer-specific parameter models (InputParams, DenseParams, Conv2DParams, etc.)
- Type-safe discrimination using `layer_type` field
- Pydantic validation for all parameters (bounds, required fields, enums)

**Graph Components:**
- `IRNode`: Layer node with parameters, position, and parent references
- `IREdge`: Directed edge connecting nodes with handle support for multi-input/output
- `IRGraph`: Complete graph with version tracking

**Validation:**
- `validate_ir_graph()`: Validates graph structure with checks for:
  - At least one input node exists
  - Concatenate nodes have 2+ incoming edges
  - No cycles (DAG constraint)
  - All edge references point to existing nodes

### 3. Graph Topology Validation (`app/ir/translator.py`)

Advanced validation logic for neural network graphs:

**Validation Rules:**
- **DAG Constraint**: Detects cycles using DFS traversal
- **Connectivity**: Ensures all nodes (except inputs) have incoming edges
- **Input Validation**: Verifies at least one input node exists
- **Merge Layer Rules**: Concatenate nodes must have 2+ inputs
- **Output Validation**: Ensures at least one node has no outgoing edges

**Functions:**
- `validate_topology(graph)`: Returns list of `IRValidationError` objects
- Detailed error reporting with node_id and field_name context

### 4. REST API Endpoints (`app/routers/layers.py`)

Three new endpoints for layer registry and graph validation:

#### `GET /layers/`
Returns the complete layer registry grouped by category

#### `GET /layers/{type_key}`
Returns a single layer specification
- Returns 404 if layer type not found
- Used for fetching detailed layer info on-demand

#### `POST /layers/validate-graph`
Validates a neural network graph

### 5. Test Coverage

Added comprehensive test suites:

#### `tests/test_registry.py`
- Registry completeness verification
- Layer specification validation
- Parameter constraint testing
- Serialization correctness

#### `tests/test_ir_validation.py`
- Valid graph structures
- Cycle detection
- Merge layer validation
- Missing input node detection
- Orphaned node detection
- Edge reference validation

prs.title: Phase 1: CI/CD Infrastructure + Layer Registry System

labels.name: needs-revision
labels.description: Reviewed; changes requested before it can land

prs.body:
## Overview

This PR establishes the foundational infrastructure for the TensorMap:

1. **CI/CD Pipeline**: GitHub Actions workflow for automated testing on every PR and push to main
2. **Layer Registry System**: Extensible, registry-driven architecture for neural network layers
3. **IR Schema**: Pydantic-based intermediate representation for neural network graphs
4. **Test Infrastructure**: Comprehensive test setup for both backend (pytest) and frontend (vitest)

## Changes

### CI/CD Infrastructure

#### `.github/workflows/test.yml`
- **Backend Tests Job**: 
  - Runs pytest with PostgreSQL 15 service container
  - Python 3.11 with pip caching via uv
  - Coverage reporting with XML artifact upload
  - Environment: `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/tensormap_test`
  
- **Frontend Tests Job**:
  - Runs vitest with coverage
  - Node.js 20 with npm caching
  - Coverage artifact upload

#### Backend Test Configuration
- **`tensormap-backend/tests/conftest.py`**: 
  - `db_session` fixture with SQLite fallback for local development
  - `client` fixture using FastAPI TestClient
  - `test_db_url` fixture reading from `DATABASE_URL` env var
  - `anyio_backend` fixture set to "asyncio"
  
- **`tensormap-backend/tests/test_ci_smoke.py`**:
  - `test_db_connection`: Verifies database connectivity with SQLModel

#### Frontend Test Configuration
- **`tensormap-frontend/src/test/setup.ts`**:
  - @testing-library/jest-dom integration
  - ResizeObserver mock (required for Recharts)
  - window.matchMedia mock (required for responsive components)
  
- **`tensormap-frontend/src/test/smoke.test.tsx`**:
  - Basic App component render test
  - Verifies React + Router + Recoil setup

### Phase 1: Layer Registry System

#### Backend: Layer Registry (`app/layers/registry.py`)

**Core Data Structures:**
- `ActivationType` enum: 8 activation functions (relu, sigmoid, tanh, softmax, softplus, selu, elu, none)
- `ParamType` enum: 4 parameter types (int, float, bool, enum)
- `ParamSpec` dataclass: Parameter specification with validation constraints (min/max, values, required, default)
- `LayerSpec` dataclass: Complete layer specification (type_key, display_name, category, keras_class, params, merge, description)

**Registry Contents (15 layers):**

| Category | Layers |
|----------|--------|
| **core** | Input, Dense, Flatten |
| **convolutional** | Conv2D |
| **pooling** | MaxPooling2D, AveragePooling2D, GlobalAveragePooling2D |
| **recurrent** | LSTM, GRU, SimpleRNN |
| **encoding** | Embedding |
| **regularization** | Dropout, BatchNormalization |
| **utility** | Reshape, Concatenate |

**Helper Functions:**
- `get_layer_spec(type_key: str) -> LayerSpec`: Retrieve layer spec by key (raises KeyError for unknown types)
- `serialize_registry() -> list[dict]`: Convert registry to JSON-serializable format for API responses

#### Backend: IR Schema (`app/ir/schema.py`)

**Pydantic Models:**
- Per-layer parameter classes (15 total): `InputParams`, `DenseParams`, `Conv2DParams`, etc.
- `NodeParams`: Discriminated union using `layer_type` field for automatic model selection
- `IRNode`: Node representation with id, node_params, position, input_node_ids
- `IREdge`: Edge representation with id, source_id, target_id, handles
- `IRGraph`: Complete graph with version, nodes, edges

**Validation:**
- `IRValidationError`: Custom exception for invalid graphs
- `validate_ir_graph(graph: IRGraph) -> None`: Validates:
  1. At least one input node exists
  2. Concatenate nodes have ≥2 incoming edges
  3. No cycles (DAG constraint)
  4. All edge references point to existing nodes

#### Frontend: GenericLayerNode Component

**`tensormap-frontend/src/components/nodes/GenericLayerNode.tsx`:**
- Universal node component for all 15 layer types
- Category-based color coding (7 categories)
- Two-letter abbreviation badges (e.g., "DN" for Dense, "LS" for LSTM)
- Dynamic parameter summary display
- Multiple input handles for merge layers (Concatenate)
- ReactFlow Handle integration (top=target, bottom=source)
- TODO comment for Phase 1 Week 2 registry data wiring

### Tests

#### Backend Tests (`tensormap-backend/tests/test_registry.py`)

**Registry Tests (8 tests):**
1. `test_all_15_layers_present`: Verifies all 15 layer types exist
2. `test_required_params_have_no_default`: Required params have `required=True` and `default=None`
3. `test_enum_params_have_values_list`: ENUM params have non-empty values list
4. `test_float_params_have_min_max`: FLOAT params have min/max constraints
5. `test_concatenate_is_merge_only_layer`: Only "concatenate" has `merge=True`
6. `test_serialize_registry_is_json_serializable`: Registry serializes to valid JSON
7. `test_get_layer_spec_success`: Helper function returns correct specs
8. `test_get_layer_spec_unknown_key`: Helper function raises KeyError for unknown types

**IR Schema Tests (7 tests):**
1. `test_ir_round_trip_dense`: Serialize/deserialize Dense node
2. `test_ir_rejects_unknown_layer_type`: Pydantic ValidationError for unknown layer_type
3. `test_validate_ir_rejects_no_input_node`: Graph without input node raises error
4. `test_validate_ir_rejects_concatenate_with_one_edge`: Concatenate with <2 edges raises error
5. `test_validate_ir_accepts_valid_graph`: Valid graph passes validation
6. `test_validate_ir_rejects_cycle`: Cyclic graph raises error
7. `test_validate_ir_rejects_nonexistent_node_reference`: Invalid edge references raise error

**Smoke Test (1 test):**
1. `test_db_connection`: Verifies database connectivity with SQLite/PostgreSQL

#### Frontend Tests (`tensormap-frontend/src/test/smoke.test.tsx`)

**Smoke Test:**
1. Renders App component without crashing
2. Verifies React + Router + Recoil setup

## Documentation Updates

### README.md
- Added CI badge: `[![CI Build Status](https://github.com/c2siorg/tensormap/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)]`
- Added "Branch Protection Rules" section with setup instructions
- Added "Development Setup" section:
  - Backend tests: `cd tensormap-backend && pytest tests/ -v`
  - Backend tests with Postgres: `export DATABASE_URL=... && pytest tests/ -v`
  - Frontend tests: `cd tensormap-frontend && npx vitest`

### CONTRIBUTING.md
Already exists with:
- Branch naming: `feat/phase-N-description`, `fix/description`, `test/description`
- Commit style: Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, test:, docs:, refactor:)
- PR size guideline: 800 lines
- Review cadence: mentor review at end of each week

prs.title: fix(backend): lazy-load TensorFlow and pin compatible backend dependencies
prs.body:
## Summary
- Replaced eager module-level TensorFlow import with lazy loading via `_get_tensorflow()` so app startup and non-ML endpoints do not fail when TensorFlow runtime is unavailable/incompatible.
- Kept module-level `tf` symbol for test monkeypatch compatibility.
- Pinned backend dependencies to avoid resolver drift causing runtime failures:
  - `tensorflow>=2.16,<2.17`
  - `protobuf>=4.25,<5`
  - `sqlmodel>=0.0.22,<0.0.23`
  - `SQLAlchemy>=2.0.37,<3`

## Why
- Backend imports crashed in test/bootstrap paths due TensorFlow/protobuf incompatibilities.
- Unpinned transitive dependency upgrades caused SQLite UUID compilation issues in tests.

## Validation
- `pip install -e ".[dev]"`
- `pytest -q`
- Result: 129 passed, 1 failed (pre-existing unrelated failure in `tests/test_model_generation.py::TestModelGeneration::test_single_input_no_edges`).

## Risk
- Dependency pinning narrows upgrade range intentionally; future upgrades should be handled in controlled PRs.

prs.title: chore(frontend): enforce supported node engine range
prs.body:
## Summary
- Added Node/NPM engine constraints to `package.json`:
  - `node >=22.12.0`
  - `npm >=11.7.0`
- Added `.nvmrc` with `22.12.0`.

## Why
- Tooling stack includes packages with strict Node engine requirements.
- This reduces environment drift and prevents hard-to-debug native/optional dependency resolution failures.

## Validation
- `npm run lint`
- Result: lint passes.

## Risk
- Developers on older Node versions must upgrade before installing/running frontend tooling.

prs.title: feat(backend): add deterministic graph auto-layout for legacy model graphs
prs.body:
## Summary
- Replaced simple vertical fallback in `_apply_auto_layout()` with deterministic layered DAG-aware positioning:
  - Builds adjacency + indegree from graph edges.
  - Uses topological layering for X positions.
  - Stacks nodes within each layer for Y positions.
  - Handles cycles/disconnected leftovers predictably.
- Preserves existing node positions; only assigns positions to nodes that do not already have one.

## Why
- Existing fallback did not reflect graph structure and had a TODO for proper layout.
- New approach improves readability and consistency of auto-positioned graphs without introducing new dependencies.

## Validation
- `ruff check app/services/deep_learning.py`
- `pytest -q tests/test_graph_json.py tests/test_deep_learning_service.py`
- Result: 23 passed.

## Risk
- Layout coordinates are deterministic but not pixel-perfect optimized like specialized graph layout engines.

prs.title: fix(api): reject unknown notification types to prevent blank emails
prs.body:
### **User description** PROBLEM: When an unknown notification type was sent to /api/send-email-notification, the handler skipped all email-building branches and sent an email with an empty subject and empty HTML body. Profile owners received blank emails. ROOT CAUSE: Lines 112-177 only built subject/html for 'profile_view' and 'payment_received' types. Any other type left both as empty strings '', and line 180 sent the email regardless. SOLUTION: Add upfront validation of the notification type against a whitelist of supported types. Unknown types are rejected with a 400 error before any email is sent. BEFORE: type='unknown_type' -> blank email sent to profile owner AFTER: type='unknown_type' -> 400 error returned, no email sent ___ ### **PR Type** Bug fix ___ ### **Description** - Adds input validation for the notification `type`. - Rejects unknown types with a `400` error. - Prevents sending blank email notifications. - **API Impact:** The `/api/send-email-notification` endpoint now returns a `400` error for unsupported types instead of sending an email. ___ ### Diagram Walkthrough ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Request to /api/send-email-notification"] --> B{Is `type` supported?}; B -- "Yes" --> C["Build and send email"]; B -- "No" --> D["Return 400 Bad Request"]; ```

File Walkthrough
Relevant files
Bug fix
send-email-notification.js
Add input validation for notification type

  • Validates the incoming notification type against the whitelist.
  • Returns a 400 error response if the type is not supported, preventing
    the email from being sent.

prs.title: fix(security): prevent Gemini API key exposure in ephemeral token end…
prs.body:
### **User description** PROBLEM: The /api/gemini-ephemeral-token endpoint returned the raw server-side Gemini API key directly in the WebSocket URL response. Any client could extract the key and abuse it for unlimited API calls. ROOT CAUSE: Line 54 embedded the actual GEMINI_API_KEY into the wsUrl string returned in the JSON response, rather than generating a short-lived ephemeral token. SOLUTION: - Use Google's newEphemeralToken endpoint to generate short-lived tokens - Never include the raw API key in any client-facing response - Fall back to server-side proxy mode if ephemeral tokens are unavailable - The raw API key now stays server-side at all times BEFORE: Response contained wsUrl with raw API key AFTER: Response contains wsUrl with ephemeral token, or proxy fallback ___ ### **PR Type** Bug fix ___ ### **Description** - Prevents exposure of the raw Gemini API key to the client. - Generates short-lived ephemeral tokens via Google's API. - Falls back to a server-side proxy if token generation fails. ___ ### Diagram Walkthrough ```mermaid flowchart LR Client["Client"] -- "POST /api/gemini-ephemeral-token" --> Server["Server API"] Server -- "Request ephemeral token" --> Google["Google API (:newEphemeralToken)"] subgraph "Success Path" Google -- "Ephemeral Token" --> Server Server -- "Returns wsUrl with token" --> Client end subgraph "Fallback Path" Google -- "Error / No Token" --> Server Server -- "Returns { useProxy: true }" --> Client end ```

File Walkthrough
Relevant files
Bug fix
gemini-ephemeral-token.js
Implement secure ephemeral token generation to prevent API key leak
api/gemini-ephemeral-token.js
  • Replaces direct API key exposure with a call to Google's
    :newEphemeralToken endpoint.
  • Introduces a requestEphemeralToken function to securely generate a
    short-lived token.
  • Implements a fallback to a server-side proxy if token generation is
    unavailable.
  • Updates the API response to return either a tokenized WebSocket URL or
    proxy instructions.

prs.title: feat(review): implement Phase 3C — Review System + Trust Score
prs.body:
Prisma Schema:
- Add Review model with @@unique([swapId, reviewerId]) constraint, rating (SmallInt 1-5), comment, isFlagged, isPublic
- Add trustScore (Decimal 5,4) and totalReviews fields to User model
- Add reviewsGiven/reviewsReceived relations on User
- Add reviews relation on Swap

ReviewService (services/review.service.js):
- submitReview: validates COMPLETED status, participant check, unique constraint (409 on duplicate), rating 1-5 range
- recalculateUserRating: Prisma _avg aggregate, async fire-and-forget
- editReview: ownership check, 24h edit window enforcement
- flagReview: admin sets isFlagged=true, excluded from avgRating
- getReviewsForUser: paginated public non-flagged reviews
- getReviewsForSwap: both reviews for a swap (participant only)

Trust Score Formula:
  trustScore = (avgRating/5 * 0.50) + (completionRate * 0.30) + (responseRate * 0.20) Recalculated on: review submitted, review edited, review flagged

Review Events (events/review.events.js):
  REVIEW_RECEIVED, REVIEW_UPDATED, REVIEW_FLAGGED

Routes:
- POST /api/swaps/:id/reviews -> submitReview
- GET /api/swaps/:id/reviews -> getReviewsForSwap
- PUT /api/reviews/:id -> editReview (owner only)
- GET /api/users/:id/reviews -> getReviewsForUser (public)
- POST /api/admin/reviews/:id/flag -> flagReview (admin only)

Tests (17 new):
- submitReview: success, duplicate 409, non-completed 400, non-participant 403, correct revieweeId, invalid rating
- recalculateUserRating: correct trust score, zero swaps
- editReview: success within 24h, fails after 24h, non-owner 403
- flagReview: excludes from rating calculation
- getReviewsForUser, getReviewsForSwap

prs.title: fix(backend): add null checks for database queries in model services
prs.body: ## Summary This PR adds comprehensive null checks to prevent `AttributeError` crashes when database records are missing. Previously, accessing attributes on `None` results from `.first()` queries caused cryptic runtime errors. ## Problem When database queries return `None` (e.g., deleted file, missing model), the code immediately accessed attributes causing: ``` AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'file_type' ``` This provided no useful information to users or developers about what was missing. ## Solution Added explicit null checks with descriptive error messages: | Location | Before | After | |----------|--------|-------| | `model_run.py:90` | `file.file_type` crashes | Returns "Dataset file not found (id=X)" | | `model_run.py:118` | `model_configs.model_type` crashes | Returns "Model configuration not found: X" | | `model_run.py:121` | `image_properties.image_size` crashes | Returns "Image properties not found for file_id=X" | | `code_generation.py:33` | `model_configs.file_id` crashes | Returns "Model not found: X" | | `code_generation.py:83` | `file.file_type` crashes | Returns "Dataset file not found (id=X)" | ## Test Plan - [x] Linting passes (`ruff check`) - [x] Formatting passes (`ruff format --check`) - [x] Python syntax valid (`py_compile`) - [x] Unit tests cover all null check scenarios ### Test Output

prs.title: fix: replace _VALID_TRANSFORMATIONS with _TRANSFORMATION_REGISTRY dispatch
prs.body:
## What

Replaces the disconnected `_VALID_TRANSFORMATIONS` set + `if/elif` chain with a single `_TRANSFORMATION_REGISTRY` dict that maps transformation names to handler functions. Adds parametrized validation tests.

## Why

4 of 7 data transformations (Min-Max Normalization, Z-score Standardization, Log Transform, Fill Missing Values) were rejected with HTTP 422 despite having working handler code. The root cause was two disconnected sources of truth: a validation set and an `if/elif` chain that could silently drift apart.

## Before → After

```python
# BEFORE: Two disconnected sources of truth
_VALID_TRANSFORMATIONS = {"One Hot Encoding", "Categorical to Numerical", "Drop Column"}
# ... handler if/elif chain 25 lines below (easily drifts)

# AFTER: Single source of truth
_TRANSFORMATION_REGISTRY = {
    "One Hot Encoding": _one_hot_encode,
    "Categorical to Numerical": _categorical_to_numerical,
    "Drop Column": _drop_column,
    "Min-Max Normalization": _min_max_normalize,
    "Z-score Standardization": _zscore_standardize,
    "Log Transform": _log_transform,
    "Fill Missing Values": _fill_missing,
}
# Validation: `if name not in _TRANSFORMATION_REGISTRY`
# Dispatch:   `_TRANSFORMATION_REGISTRY[name](df, col, params)`
```

## Safety

- All 7 handler functions preserve identical behavior - extracted verbatim from the original `if/elif` branches
- Backward compatible - same transformation names, same API contract
- Matches frontend UI list in `TransformationCreator.jsx`
- Unknown names still rejected with 422
- Structurally prevents this class of bug from recurring
- Ruff lint + format passing

Fixes #204

prs.title: feat: add model architecture summary panel (#145)
prs.body:
## Summary

When designing a neural network on the TensorMap canvas users had no visibility into model complexity — parameter count, per-layer output shapes, or the trainable/non-trainable breakdown. This PR adds a `_build_model_summary()` backend helper that extracts structured summary data from the already-built Keras model during validation/save, and a collapsible `ModelSummaryPanel` on the frontend that displays it after successful validation.

## Root Cause (Gap Analysis)

| Gap | Detail |
|---|---|
| No summary extraction | `model_save_service` and `model_validate_service` both instantiate a `tf.keras.Model` but discard it — the `data` field of every success response was `None` |
| No frontend display | `Canvas.jsx` only checked `resp.success` and showed a dialog; `resp.data` was never read |
| No component | Nothing in the component tree could render layer-level stats |

## Design Decisions

- **Reuse the already-built `keras_model`**: Both service functions already call `tf.keras.models.model_from_json()` to validate the model. The result was previously thrown away. Assigning it to a variable costs nothing and enables summary extraction.
- **`data.summary` in response, not a new endpoint**: The summary is produced during validation/save. Adding a separate `/model/summary` endpoint would require re-building the Keras model on each call. Embedding it in the existing success response is simpler and faster.
- **Collapsible, closeable panel below canvas**: Matches the existing `Card`-based layout. Closing clears React state — no re-validation needed. Collapsing hides only the table body, keeping the header visible.
- **`modelSummary` cleared on save failure**: Avoids showing stale summary data from a previous attempted model.


## Backward Compatibility

- The `data` field in success responses was previously `null` — any client that ignored `data` is unaffected; the field now carries `{"summary": {...}}`
- Error responses are completely unchanged
- No new endpoints, no schema changes, no DB migrations required

## Testing

- Save a valid canvas model → summary panel appears below canvas with per-layer rows and param totals matching Keras's `model.summary()` output
- Collapse → header remains visible, table hidden
- Close → panel disappears; re-saving shows a fresh summary
- Invalid model save → panel is not shown (or previous panel cleared)

Closes #145

prs.title: feat: store ReactFlow graph as JSON column in model_basic (#140)
prs.body:
Closes #140 

## Summary

When a user designs a neural network on the ReactFlow canvas and validates it, the original graph structure (nodes, edges, positions, parameters) was never persisted. Only a compiled Keras JSON file was saved to disk, making it impossible to reload the architecture back onto the canvas, compare models by structure, or query the graph at the database level. This PR adds a `graph_json` column to `model_basic` and stores the full ReactFlow graph at validation/save time.

## Root Cause

Three compounding gaps:

| Gap | Detail |
|---|---|
| No DB column | `ModelBasic` had nowhere to store the raw graph |
| Graph never captured | Both `model_validate_service` and `model_save_service` had the full dict in `incoming["model"]` / `incoming` but never wrote it anywhere |
| Positions silently stripped | `GraphNode` schema had no `position` field — Pydantic discarded `position` from every node before the service layer, making canvas reload impossible |

The existing `get_model_graph_service` worked around the missing column by reconstructing the graph from flattened `ModelConfigs` key-value rows, a lossy and complex operation that could not recover node positions.

## Design Decisions

- **JSON column over a separate table** — The graph is a document (hierarchical, variable-shape). A single nullable JSON column is simpler, queryable at the DB level, and avoids an extra join.
- **Nullable column** — Existing rows are completely unaffected. The service falls back to the legacy `ModelConfigs` reconstruction for any row where `graph_json IS NULL`.
- **`position` field on `GraphNode`** — Without this field in the Pydantic schema, node positions are stripped before reaching the service. Making it optional preserves full backward compatibility with clients that omit it.
- **Fast path / legacy fallback** — `get_model_graph_service` checks `model.graph_json` first (single column read). If `NULL`, it transparently falls back to the existing `_unflatten_model_configs` logic — zero regression risk for pre-migration models.

## Changes in Detail

### `app/models/ml.py`
Added `graph_json: dict | None = Field(default=None, sa_column=Column(JSON, nullable=True))` to `ModelBasic`, with `JSON` imported from `sqlalchemy`.

### Migration `d4b7f1a03e82`
Purely additive — adds a nullable JSON column. `downgrade()` drops it cleanly.

### `app/schemas/deep_learning.py`
```python
class GraphNode(BaseModel):
    id: str
    type: str
    data: NodeData
    position: dict[str, Any] | None = None   # ← new
```

### `app/services/deep_learning.py`

**`model_validate_service`** — stores the graph on creation:
```python
model = ModelBasic(
    ...
    graph_json=incoming["model"],   # ← new
)
```

**`model_save_service`** — stores the graph on save:
```python
model = ModelBasic(
    model_name=model_name,
    project_id=project_id,
    graph_json=incoming,            # ← new
)
```

**`get_model_graph_service`** — fast path for new models, legacy fallback for old:
```python
if model.graph_json is not None:
    graph = model.graph_json
    # auto-layout fallback for any node lacking position
    ...
    return graph   # direct — no reconstruction needed

# legacy: reconstruct from ModelConfigs as before
```

**`get_available_model_list`** — richer response:
```python
data = [{"model_name": m.model_name, "graph_json": m.graph_json} for m in models]
```

### `Training.jsx`
Updated `getAllModels` mapping to consume the new object shape:
```js
const models = response.map((item, index) => ({
    label: item.model_name + strings.MODEL_EXTENSION,
    value: item.model_name,
    key: index,
}));
```

prs.title: fix: wire batch_size parameter through full training stack (#127)
prs.body:
### Summary

Closes #127

The `batch_size` field was collected from the user on the Training page but silently dropped before ever reaching `model.fit()`. TensorFlow always used its default of 32 regardless of what was entered. This PR wires `batch_size` through every layer of the stack so the user's value is actually respected.

### Root Cause

`batch_size` was lost at four independent points:

| Layer | Problem |
|-------|---------|
| `Training.jsx` `handleSaveConfig` | `batch_size` existed in state but was never included in the payload sent to the API |
| `TrainingConfigRequest` schema | No `batch_size` field — Pydantic would reject or ignore any value sent |
| `update_training_config_service` | Never assigned `config["batch_size"]` to `model.batch_size` |
| `ModelBasic` DB model | No `batch_size` column — nothing to persist or read back in `model_run.py` |

### Changes

#### Backend

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `app/models/ml.py` | Added `batch_size: int \| None` nullable column to `ModelBasic` |
| `migrations/versions/c3a1d9e02f45_...py` | Alembic migration to add the column (`down_revision = "f1a2b3c4d5e6"`, the current single head on main) |
| `app/schemas/deep_learning.py` | Added `batch_size: int = Field(default=32, gt=0)` to `TrainingConfigRequest` |
| `app/services/deep_learning.py` | `update_training_config_service` now persists `model.batch_size = config.get("batch_size", 32)` |
| `app/services/model_run.py` | Reads `model_configs.batch_size` (falls back to 32 for legacy rows) and passes it to `model.fit()` in the tabular path |

#### Frontend

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `Training.jsx` | `handleSaveConfig` now includes `batch_size: trainingConfig.batch_size ? Number(trainingConfig.batch_size) : 32` in the PATCH request payload |

### Backward Compatibility

- Existing model rows with `batch_size = NULL` fall back to **32** at runtime — identical behaviour to before
- API clients that omit `batch_size` receive the default of 32 via Pydantic's field default
- Migration column is nullable so `alembic upgrade head` is non-breaking for existing data
- No breaking changes to any existing endpoint or schema

### Testing

- Manually verified the field flows end-to-end: form → PATCH request → DB → `model.fit()`
- Setting `batch_size=16` vs `batch_size=128` on the same dataset produces visibly different epoch durations and steps-per-epoch counts in the Socket.IO progress output

prs.title: Decouple data initialization from disk I/O with buffered batch writes (#60)
prs.body:
Closes #60

## Problem

The `Case.createCase()` method in `CaseClass.py` uses an **Aggressive Write-Through** pattern. Each of the 23 `default_*` methods (R, RY, RT, RE, RS, RYT, RYTM, RYTEM, etc.) builds a dictionary and immediately calls `File.writeFile()`, resulting in:

1. **High latency** - 20+ sequential blocking I/O operations per case creation. In models with high dimensionality (many Technologies × Years × Scenarios), the cumulative disk wait blocks the Flask event loop.
2. **No atomicity** - If a failure occurs mid-sequence (e.g., disk full on the 15th write), the case directory is left in a partially-initialized state a "Zombie Case" that corrupts the UI.

```python
# BEFORE: each method immediately writes to disk
def default_R(self):
    Rdata = { ... }  # build data
    File.writeFile(Rdata, self.jsonPath['R'])  # blocking I/O

def createCase(self):
    for group, array in self.PARAMETERS.items():
        if array:
            func = getattr(self, Config.DEFAULT_F[group])
            func()  # each call = 1 disk write
```

## Solution

Introduce a **Deferred I/O Strategy** with a 3-phase lifecycle in `createCase()`:

### Phase 1 - Aggregate (pure functions)

All 23 `default_*` methods are converted to **pure functions**: they build and **return** their data dictionaries without touching the filesystem. The data generation logic is completely unchanged.

```diff
 def default_R(self):
     ...
-        File.writeFile( Rdata, self.jsonPath['R'])
+        return Rdata
```

### Phase 2 -  Commit (buffered batch write)

`createCase()` collects all returned data into an in-memory buffer, then flushes everything to disk in a single batch:

```python
def createCase(self):
    # Phase 1: Aggregate
    buffer = {}
    for group, array in self.PARAMETERS.items():
        if array:
            func = getattr(self, Config.DEFAULT_F[group])
            buffer[group] = func()

    # Phase 2: Commit
    written = []
    try:
        for group, data in buffer.items():
            File.writeFile(data, self.jsonPath[group])
            written.append(self.jsonPath[group])
    except (IOError, OSError):
        # Phase 3: Rollback
        for path in written:
            try:
                os.remove(path)
            except OSError:
                pass
        raise IOError
```

### Phase 3 - Rollback (transactional integrity)

If any write fails during the commit phase, all already-written files are removed via `os.remove()`, and `IOError` is re-raised. This prevents zombie cases with partially-written parameter files.

## Why this approach

- **Minimal diff** - Only the I/O side effect is removed from each method. All data generation logic stays identical.
- **Backward compatible** - The public API of `createCase()` is unchanged. Both callers (`CaseRoute.py` line 355 and `ImportTemplate.py` line 833) call `case.createCase()` with no return value and expect `IOError` on failure this contract is preserved exactly.
- **No new dependencies** - Uses only `os.remove()` from stdlib (already available).
- **UpdateCaseClass unaffected** - The `update_*` methods are in a separate class and are not touched.

## Tests

Added `tests/test_case_class.py` with 9 tests across 3 classes:

### `TestDefaultMethodsArePure` (4 tests)
- `default_R()` returns a dict with correct parameter keys
- `default_R()` populates SC_0 with defaults and SC_1 with None
- `default_RY()` returns a dict
- Calling `default_*` methods does **not** create any files on disk

### `TestCreateCaseBatchWrite` (3 tests)
- `createCase()` creates expected JSON files (R.json, RY.json)
- Written file content matches what `default_*` methods return
- Empty parameter groups produce no file

### `TestRollbackOnFailure` (2 tests)
- Simulated `IOError` on 2nd write → all partial files are cleaned up
- Case directory itself survives rollback (caller owns it)

```
$ pytest tests/ -v
============================== 12 passed in 1.32s ==============================
```

All 12 tests pass (9 new + 3 existing `test_shutdown.py`). Zero regressions.


## How to test

```bash
# Run full test suite
pytest tests/ -v

# Verify no File.writeFile calls remain in default_* methods
grep -n "File.writeFile" API/Classes/Case/CaseClass.py
# Expected: only the comment on line 31 and the batch-write in createCase()
```

prs.title: Optional macOS local UX: graceful Quit action and launcher readiness handling

labels.name: needs-intake-fix
labels.description: PR intake structure needs maintainer follow-up

prs.body:
Closes #63

## Problem

Local contributors running MUIOGO on macOS have two UX pain points:

1. **No way to stop the app from the UI** - users must manually `Ctrl+C` or `kill` the terminal process to shut down the backend.
2. **No launcher script exists** - if one were introduced with a fixed `sleep` before opening the browser, it would be unreliable across machines with different startup times.

## Solution

Three incremental, opt-in additions - all guarded by `HEROKU_DEPLOY == 0` / `Base.HEROKU === 0` so **deployed environments are completely unaffected**.

### 1. Backend: `/shutdown` route (local-only)

Registered conditionally at module level. Sends `SIGINT` to the current process for a clean waitress server shutdown the same signal `Ctrl+C` sends.

```diff
+import signal
 ...
+#graceful shutdown for local mode only
+if Config.HEROKU_DEPLOY == 0:
+    @app.route("/shutdown", methods=['POST'])
+    def shutdown():
+        os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGINT)
+        return jsonify({"message": "Server shutting down..."}), 200
```

**Why `SIGINT` over `werkzeug.server.shutdown`?** The project uses waitress in local mode, not werkzeug's dev server, so `SIGINT` is the correct portable shutdown signal.

### 2. Frontend: `Base.quit()` + sidebar Quit button

- `Base.quit()` shows a native `confirm()` dialog, then POSTs to `/shutdown`.
- On success (or connection error, since the server is dying), replaces the page with a "stopped" message.
- Sidebar `<li id="quit-app">` is hidden by default and shown via a `<script type="module">` that checks `Base.HEROKU === 0`.

```diff
+    static quit() {
+        if (this.HEROKU === 1) return;
+        if (!confirm('Are you sure you want to quit MUIOGO?')) return;
+        $.ajax({
+            url: this.apiUrl() + "shutdown",
+            type: 'POST',
+            success: function () { ... },
+            error: function () { ... }
+        });
+    }
```

### 3. Launcher script: `start_muiogo.sh`

Starts the backend in the background, then polls `http://127.0.0.1:5002` every second with a 30-second timeout before opening the browser. Detects early process exit and handles all three platforms (macOS `open`, Linux `xdg-open`, fallback message).

```bash
while ! curl -s -o /dev/null "${URL}" 2>/dev/null; do
    # check process alive, check timeout, sleep 1
done
```

## Tests

Added `tests/test_shutdown.py` with 3 cases:

- `POST /shutdown` returns 200 with message and calls `os.kill`
- `POST /shutdown` sends `SIGINT` to its own PID
- `GET /shutdown` is rejected (404/405)

```
$ pytest tests/ -v
7 passed in 0.84s
```

All existing tests continue to pass.

## How to test manually

```bash
# Option A: Use the launcher
./start_muiogo.sh
# → Browser opens automatically when server is ready
# → Click "Quit" in the sidebar to stop

# Option B: Start manually
python API/app.py
# → Open http://127.0.0.1:5002
# → Click "Quit" in the sidebar → confirm → server stops cleanly
```

prs.title: Fix cleanup failure when non-directory files exist in results path
prs.body: Closes #62 ## Problem On macOS, Finder drops `.DS_Store` files inside `run/result` folders. The `cleanUp()` loop in `DataFileClass.py` iterates entries under `self.resultsPath` and calls `os.listdir()` on each one, assuming they're all directories. When a regular file like `.DS_Store` is present, that call throws `NotADirectoryError` and the whole cleanup aborts. Same class of issue as upstream OSeMOSYS#7. ## Fix Before descending into each entry, guard with `os.path.isdir()`. Stray files get removed as part of cleanup instead of crashing it. Error handling follows the same pattern already used in the inner loop. ```diff for caserunname in os.listdir(self.resultsPath): caserunname_path = os.path.join(self.resultsPath, caserunname) + if not os.path.isdir(caserunname_path): + try: + os.remove(caserunname_path) + except Exception as e: + print(f"Greška pri brisanju {caserunname_path}: {e}") + continue for carerunData in os.listdir(caserunname_path): ``` 6 lines added, 0 removed. `os.path.isdir()` works the same on macOS, Linux, and Windows, so no platform-specific behavior. ## Tests Added `tests/test_cleanup.py` with 4 cases: - `.DS_Store` at results root → cleanup completes, file removed, subdirectory contents cleaned - Directory-only results → no regression, works as before - Multiple stray files (`.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `.gitkeep`) → all removed - View folder cleanup → `resData.json` preserved, other files deleted ``` $ pytest tests/ -v 29 passed in 1.59s ```

prs.title: feat: add model deletion with cascade cleanup
prs.body:
Closes #133

## Summary

TensorMap previously had no way to delete saved models, causing the model list to accumulate stale entries over time.

This PR introduces a complete full-stack delete capability:
	•	Backend: DELETE /model/{id} endpoint with cascade cleanup
	•	Frontend: Per-model delete button with confirmation dialog in the Training UI

## Changes

### Backend

**`tensormap-backend/app/services/deep_learning.py`**
- Added `delete_model_service(db, model_id)`: looks up `ModelBasic` by integer PK, deletes it (the existing `cascade="all,delete"` on the `configs` relationship automatically removes all associated `ModelConfigs` rows), and does a best-effort removal of the model JSON file from disk.
- Updated `get_available_model_list()` to return `{ id, model_name }` objects instead of bare strings, so the frontend can issue DELETE requests using the numeric DB id.

**`tensormap-backend/app/routers/deep_learning.py`**
- Added `DELETE /model/{model_id}` route backed by `delete_model_service`.

**`tensormap-backend/tests/test_deep_learning_service.py`** *(new)*
- 5 unit tests for `delete_model_service` using `MagicMock` — no live DB or TensorFlow required:
  - `test_success_deletes_model_and_file` — happy path, JSON file is removed
  - `test_model_not_found_returns_404` — returns 404 when id is unknown
  - `test_success_when_json_file_missing` — deletion still succeeds if JSON file is already gone
  - `test_db_error_rolls_back` — DB exception triggers rollback and returns 500
  - `test_get_called_with_correct_id` — verifies `db.get(ModelBasic, id)` call signature

**`tensormap-backend/tests/__init__.py`** *(new)* — makes `tests/` a proper Python package.

### Frontend

**`tensormap-frontend/src/constants/Urls.js`**
- Added `BACKEND_DELETE_MODEL = "/model"` constant.

**`tensormap-frontend/src/services/ModelServices.js`**
- Updated `getAllModels()` to handle the new `{ id, model_name }` response shape.
- Added `deleteModel(modelId)` — issues `DELETE /model/{modelId}`.

**`tensormap-frontend/src/containers/Training/Training.jsx`**
- Added `deleteConfirm` state, `handleDeleteClick`, and `handleDeleteConfirm` handlers.
- Renders a `<Trash2>` icon button next to each model in the dropdown.
- Clicking the trash icon opens a confirmation dialog showing the model name.
- Confirming calls `deleteModel`, removes the entry from the Recoil atom, and clears `selectedModel` if the deleted model was active.
- Cancelling closes the dialog with no side effects.

**`tensormap-frontend/src/components/DragAndDropCanvas/Canvas.jsx`**
- Updated `loadModel()` to consume the new `{ id, model_name }` shape from `getAllModels()`.
- Replaced the optimistic `setModelList` push after save with a re-fetch of `getAllModels()` so every entry in the list carries a valid DB id (needed for deletion).


## Testing

```bash
# Backend unit tests (no DB / TF required)
cd tensormap-backend
uv run pytest tests/test_deep_learning_service.py -v
# 5 passed
```

prs.title: feat: add full activation function options to Conv2D and Dense nodes
prs.body:
Closes #129

## Summary

Expands the activation function dropdown for the Conv2D node from the two hardcoded options (none, relu) to the full set of commonly used Keras activation functions: none, relu, sigmoid, tanh, softmax, elu, selu.

For consistency, the Dense node dropdown is unified to use the same list. A single shared `ACTIVATIONS` constant replaces the two separate `denseActivations` and `convActivations` arrays (DRY). The backend already maps `"none"` → `"linear"` for Conv2D; the same mapping is now applied to Dense so both layer types behave correctly when `"none"` is selected.

## Changes

### Root Cause
`convActivations` in `NodePropertiesPanel.jsx` only contained `["none", "relu"]`. The backend had no issues  it passes the activation string directly to `tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(activation=…)`, which natively supports all standard Keras names.

### Frontend
- **`NodePropertiesPanel.jsx`** - Replaced separate `denseActivations` and `convActivations` arrays with a single `ACTIVATIONS` constant containing all 7 options. Both the Dense and Conv2D `<Select>` dropdowns now render from this shared list.

### Backend
- **`model_generation.py`** - Added `"none"` → `"linear"` mapping for the `customdense` branch in `_build_layer()` to match the existing Conv2D handling.



## How to Test

```bash
# Frontend - start dev server and verify dropdowns
cd tensormap-frontend
npm install
npm run dev
# Open canvas, add a Conv2D node, open properties panel - confirm 7 activation options appear
# Repeat for Dense node

# Backend - run existing tests
cd tensormap-backend
uv run pytest -v
```

prs.title: test: add pytest suite for data processing service
prs.body:
## Summary

Adds a comprehensive unit test suite for `app/services/data_process.py` - the service responsible for CSV ingestion, target field management, metrics computation, and data preprocessing. Bugs in this layer can silently corrupt training data, making test coverage especially valuable.

All tests use `MagicMock` for the SQLModel `Session` (no real database required) and pytest's `tmp_path` fixture for CSV file creation.

---


## Test Coverage (18 tests)

| Service Function | # Tests | Scenarios Covered |
|---|---|---|
| `add_target_service` | 2 | success (201), file not found (400) |
| `get_one_target_by_id_service` | 3 | success (200), file not found (400), target record missing (400) |
| `delete_one_target_by_id_service` | 3 | success (200), file not found (400), target record missing (400) |
| `get_all_targets_service` | 1 | paginated results with correct metadata |
| `get_data_metrics` | 3 | success (200), file not in DB (400), CSV missing on disk (500) |
| `get_file_data` | 2 | success with JSON records (200), file not in DB (400) |
| `preprocess_data` | 4 | file not in DB (400), one-hot encoding, categorical-to-numerical, drop column |

---

## Approach

- **MagicMock for DB session** - As agreed, `sqlmodel.Session` is mocked using `MagicMock`. Multi-call sequences (e.g. lookup file then lookup target record) use `side_effect` lists so successive `.exec().first()` calls return different values.
- **`tmp_path` for CSV files** - Realistic sample CSVs are written to pytest's temporary directories; no fixtures depend on the real filesystem or upload folder.
- **`get_settings` patched at import site** - Since `get_settings` is `lru_cache`-decorated, it is patched at `app.services.data_process.get_settings` to redirect `upload_folder` to `tmp_path` without polluting the cache.
- **No external dependencies** - Tests do not require a running database, network access, or the TensorFlow stack to be initialised.

---

## How to run

```bash
cd tensormap-backend
uv run pytest tests/test_data_process_service.py -v
```

Closes #123

prs.title: fix(auth): extract missing `purpose` field in set-password endpoint
prs.body:
## Problem Statement(Issue fix #514 )

The `/api/auth/set-password` endpoint crashes with `NameError: name 'purpose' is not defined` on every request. The variable `purpose` is used in conditional logic (`if purpose == "signup":`) but is never extracted from the request body. This makes the **entire password-reset flow** and the **OTP-based signup completion flow** non-functional users who forget their password are permanently locked out.

## Root Cause

When the `set-password` handler was written, the `purpose` field extraction was accidentally omitted from the `try` block that parses `email` and `password`. The frontend (`ForgotPassword.tsx`, `SignUp.tsx`) correctly sends `purpose` in the JSON body, but the server never reads it.

## Solution Summary

| Change | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Extract `purpose` | Added `purpose = sanitize_string(data.get("purpose"), field_name="purpose")` in the validation block |
| Validate `purpose` | Added guard: `if purpose not in ("signup", "reset"): return 400` |
| Password length check | Added `len(password) < 8` guard consistent with `complete-signup` endpoint |
| Tests | Created `tests/test_set_password.py` with 8 test cases |

## Testing Proof

8 new test cases covering:

| Test | Validates |
|------|-----------|
| `test_set_password_missing_purpose` | The crash scenario → now returns 400 |
| `test_set_password_invalid_purpose` | Rejects unknown purpose values |
| `test_set_password_missing_email` | Missing email → 400 |
| `test_set_password_missing_password` | Missing password → 400 |
| `test_set_password_short_password` | Password < 8 chars → 400 |
| `test_set_password_reset_success` | Happy path for password reset → 200 + token |
| `test_set_password_reset_user_not_found` | Reset for non-existent user → 404 |
| `test_set_password_signup_success` | Happy path for OTP signup → 200 + token |
| `test_set_password_signup_duplicate_email` | Duplicate email → 400 |

All tests pass with the existing backend test suite (27 total).

prs.title: fix: deduplicate export presence logic in Harmony dependency classes
prs.body: ### Issue(Fixes #20523 ) ## Problem Three parser plugins independently compute `exportPresenceMode` from parser options using an identical ternary chain: ```js this.exportPresenceMode = options.importExportsPresence !== undefined // or reexportExportsPresence ? ExportPresenceModes.fromUserOption(options.importExportsPresence) : options.exportsPresence !== undefined ? ExportPresenceModes.fromUserOption(options.exportsPresence) : options.strictExportPresence ? ExportPresenceModes.ERROR : ExportPresenceModes.AUTO; ``` This pattern is duplicated in: - `HarmonyImportDependencyParserPlugin` (using `importExportsPresence`) - `HarmonyExportDependencyParserPlugin` (using `reexportExportsPresence`) - A future callsite would need to copy the same chain again A second duplication: `getNonOptionalPart(members, membersOptionals)`, a small utility that strips optional-chaining tails from member chains, was defined identically inside both `HarmonyImportDependencyParserPlugin.apply()` and `ImportParserPlugin.apply()`. ## Approach 1. Add `ExportPresenceModes.resolveFromOptions(specificValue, options)` — a single method on the existing `ExportPresenceModes` object that encapsulates the three-tier fallback (`specificValue` → `exportsPresence` → `strictExportPresence`). Each plugin now calls `resolveFromOptions(options.importExportsPresence, options)` or `resolveFromOptions(options.reexportExportsPresence, options)`. 2. Move `getNonOptionalPart` into `HarmonyImportDependency.js` as a named export. Both parser plugins now `require` it from there instead of defining their own copy. prs.title: Add cross-platform setup flow for full MUIOGO environment
prs.body:
Closes #8

## What changed and why

A new contributor currently has no single command to go from a fresh clone to a runnable MUIOGO install. Python environment creation, dependency installation, solver binary setup, and verification are all manual and undocumented. This PR adds a single cross-platform setup flow that handles everything.

### New files

#### `scripts/setup_dev.py` — Main setup script

A pure-Python script (no third-party dependencies needed to run it) that executes four steps:

| Step | What it does |
|------|-------------|
| **1. Python venv** | Creates a `venv/` virtual environment using the stdlib `venv` module. Skips if one already exists. |
| **2. Python dependencies** | Upgrades pip, then runs `pip install -r requirements.txt` inside the venv. |
| **3. Solver dependencies** | Installs GLPK and CBC via the OS package manager — Homebrew (macOS), apt/dnf/pacman (Linux), or Chocolatey (Windows). Skips solvers already on PATH. |
| **4. Verification checks** | Validates everything with clear `[PASS]`/`[FAIL]` output for each item (see details below). |

**Verification checks performed:**
- Python venv exists and its interpreter is reachable
- 7 core Python packages import successfully (`flask`, `flask_cors`, `pandas`, `numpy`, `openpyxl`, `waitress`, `dotenv`)
- `glpsol` binary found and version prints
- `cbc` binary found and version prints (uses `echo "quit" | cbc` to avoid CBC's interactive prompt hang)
- Flask app module loads without import errors

**Failure handling:**
- Each step reports pass/fail independently — a solver install failure does not block Python setup
- Failed solver installs print manual installation commands for all supported platforms
- Final summary shows all results and prints actionable next steps

**Modes:**
- `python scripts/setup_dev.py` — full setup (create venv + install deps + install solvers + verify)
- `python scripts/setup_dev.py --check` — verification only (skip install steps)

**Design decisions:**
- Uses `venv` (stdlib) instead of conda — lowers onboarding friction, no extra tooling needed
- Pure Python with no external dependencies — the script itself runs before any venv exists
- No solver binaries committed to the repo — installed via OS package managers only
- Terminal color output auto-disabled when not in a TTY (CI-safe)

#### `scripts/setup.sh` — Bash wrapper (macOS / Linux)

Detects a suitable Python 3.9+ interpreter and delegates to `setup_dev.py`. Provides a cleaner entry point:
```bash
./scripts/setup.sh          # full setup
./scripts/setup.sh --check  # verify only
```

#### `scripts/setup.bat` — Batch wrapper (Windows)

Same as above for Windows:
```cmd
scripts\setup.bat
scripts\setup.bat --check
```

## Platform support

| Platform | Package manager | Tested |
|----------|----------------|--------|
| macOS | Homebrew | Yes (arm64, Python 3.12) |
| Ubuntu/Debian | apt-get | Supported (not tested locally) |
| Fedora/RHEL | dnf | Supported (not tested locally) |
| Arch Linux | pacman | Supported (not tested locally) |
| Windows | Chocolatey | Supported (not tested locally) |

## Validation

Full setup run on macOS (clean venv):

prs.title: fix(backend): use sync route handlers to prevent event loop blocking
prs.body:
## Problem (Fixes #113)

All route handlers are declared `async def`, but every service they call performs synchronous blocking operations (`pd.read_csv()`, `db.exec()`, `model.fit()`, `file.read()`). In `async def` handlers, blocking calls freeze the entire uvicorn event loop — no other request can be served. A single model training run via `POST /model/run` makes the server completely unresponsive to all users until training finishes.

## Changes

### All routers (`data_upload.py`, `data_process.py`, `deep_learning.py`, `project.py`)
- Changed all route handlers from `async def` to plain `def`
- FastAPI automatically runs sync handlers in a threadpool (`anyio.to_thread.run_sync()`), preventing event loop stalls
- No other code changes needed — return values and dependencies remain the same

### Why `def` instead of `async def`?

FastAPI's documentation explicitly recommends:
- Use `async def` only when your code uses `await` (async DB drivers, httpx, etc.)
- Use plain `def` when calling synchronous/blocking code — FastAPI runs it in a threadpool automatically

Since all services use synchronous SQLModel/SQLAlchemy, synchronous pandas, and synchronous TensorFlow, plain `def` is the correct choice.

## Testing
1. Start uvicorn with `--workers 1`
2. Trigger `POST /model/run` with a model that takes >5s to train
3. While training, send `GET /project` from another terminal
4. **Before fix:** The GET request hangs until training completes
5. **After fix:** The GET request responds immediately

prs.title: perf(backend): cache CSV column names in DB instead of reading from disk on every list call
prs.body:
## Problem(Fixes #110)

`get_all_files_service()` iterates over every `DataFile` record and calls `pd.read_csv()` on each CSV to extract column names. This causes:

- **O(n × file_size) disk I/O per request** — every list-files API call reads every CSV from disk
- **Event loop blocking** — synchronous pandas I/O inside async handlers
- **Fragility** — one missing/corrupt file on disk breaks listing for all files (returns 500)
- **No caching** — column names never change after upload, yet they're re-read every time

## Changes

### `app/models/data.py`
- Add nullable `columns: List[str] | None` JSON field to `DataFile` model

### `app/services/data_upload.py`
- **`add_file_service()`**: Extract column names from CSV at upload time and store in the `columns` field
- **`get_all_files_service()`**: Read `columns` from the DB record instead of `pd.read_csv()` on every call. Falls back to disk read only for existing files without cached columns, and backfills the DB record on first access.

### Migration
- `f1a2b3c4d5e6_add_columns_json_to_data_file.py`: Adds `columns` JSON column to `data_file` table (nullable, backward-compatible)

## Testing

1. Upload a CSV → verify `columns` is populated in the DB
2. `GET /data/upload/file` → should return column names without reading from disk
3. Existing files without cached columns → should be backfilled on first list call
4. Upload a ZIP → `columns` should be `null` (no CSV to parse)

prs.title: [BUG] Fix GroupNormalizer clone compatibility in pytorch-forecasting …
prs.body:
#### Reference Issue

Fixes #9366

#### What does this implement/fix?

`GroupNormalizer` from `pytorch-forecasting` cannot be cloned using `sktime.base.clone()`. This is because `GroupNormalizer`'s constructor **mutates** its `method_kwargs` argument in-place. When `sktime`'s `clone()` mechanism calls `get_params()` and then reconstructs the object with those parameters, the mutated `method_kwargs` dict causes corruption.

This critically breaks any `sktime` forecaster that uses `GroupNormalizer` with internal cloning, including `Reconciler`, benchmarking, and grid search.

**Root cause**: The `_PytorchForecastingAdapter.__init__()` stored the user-provided `dataset_params` dict directly (by reference). When `GroupNormalizer` inside that dict mutated its arguments, subsequent clone operations would receive already-mutated parameters.

**Fix**: `deepcopy` the `dataset_params` at two points:
1. At adapter `__init__` time - so the user's original objects are never mutated
2. Before passing to `TimeSeriesDataSet` in `_Xy_to_dataset()` - so each fit/predict call gets a clean copy

#### Does my contribution introduce a new dependency?

No. (`deepcopy` from `copy` was already imported in the file.)

#### Did I add any tests for the change?

Yes, `test_adapter_deepcopies_dataset_params` creates a mock normalizer class that mutates constructor arguments (simulating `GroupNormalizer` behavior) and verifies the original user-provided dict is NOT corrupted after adapter construction.

The test uses `pytest.importorskip("pytorch_forecasting")` to skip gracefully when the soft dependency is not installed.

prs.title: [BUG] Add input validation and safety restrictions to craft function …
prs.body:
#### Reference Issue

Fixes #9430

#### What does this implement/fix?

The `craft()` function in `sktime/registry/_craft.py` uses `eval()` and `exec()` to construct objects from string specifications without any input validation or sanitization. While primarily used for internal deserialization, `craft` is a public API, making it a potential security concern if untrusted strings are ever passed to it.

This PR adds three layers of defense:

1. **Type check**: Rejects non-string inputs with `TypeError`
2. **Pattern blocklist**: Rejects specs containing known dangerous patterns (`__import__`, `subprocess`, `os.system`, `__subclasses__`, `__builtins__`, `__globals__`, `shutil.rmtree`) with `ValueError`
3. **Restricted builtins**: Removes dangerous built-in functions (`__import__`, `open`, `exec`, `eval`, `compile`, `breakpoint`) from the execution context while preserving all safe builtins needed for legitimate estimator construction
4. **Documentation**: Adds security warnings to both the module docstring and the `craft` function docstring, along with proper `Raises` and `Warnings` sections

#### Does my contribution introduce a new dependency?

No.

#### Did I add any tests for the change?

Yes, two new tests:
- `test_craft_rejects_non_string`: Verifies `TypeError` for non-string inputs (`int`, `None`)
- `test_craft_rejects_unsafe_patterns`: Parametrized test with 4 unsafe pattern variants (`__import__`, `__subclasses__`, `__builtins__`, `__globals__`)

All existing passing tests continue to pass with the restricted builtins.

prs.title: [BUG] Add encoding='utf-8' to JSONStorageHandler file operations (#9431)
prs.body:
#### Reference Issues/PRs

Fixes #9431

#### What does this implement/fix?

`JSONStorageHandler` opens files without explicitly specifying the `encoding` parameter in `open()` calls. Without this, Python uses the platform default encoding (e.g., `cp1252` on Windows), which causes `UnicodeDecodeError` for non-ASCII characters in benchmark results (model IDs, score names, etc.).

This PR adds `encoding="utf-8"` to both `open()` calls in `JSONStorageHandler.save()` and `_load()`, ensuring consistent cross-platform behavior.


#### Did you add any tests for the change?

Yes, `test_json_storage_handler_unicode` verifies round-trip save/load of results with unicode characters in `model_id`, `task_id`, and score names.
